title: Insulating a suspended timber floor from below
subtitle: Mineral wool batts fitted between joists through the subfloor void
excerpt: Pre-1950 suspended timber ground floors sit over a ventilated void. Fitting 100 mm mineral wool batts between the joists from below cuts the floor U-value from around 0.8 to under 0.25 W/m²·K. Access is via a removed airbrick or subfloor hatch. Materials for one room cost .
sourceNotes: Energy Saving Trust floor insulation guidance. BRE BR 443 floor U-value reference tables. CIGA (Cavity Insulation Guarantee Agency) suspended floor guidance. NHBC Standards Chapter 6.3 subfloor ventilation requirements.

recipeTools.notes: Long blade for cutting mineral wool batts to length and width.
For cutting timber noggins to retain batts.
For fixing retaining laths or stapling netting.

glossaryTerms.definition: Glass fibre or rock fibre insulation. Available as rolls, batts, or loose fill. Lambda 0.032–0.044 W/m·K. Not moisture-sensitive, rodent-resistant, non-combustible at standard densities. Used in loft, wall, and floor insulation.

content.text: A suspended timber ground floor with no insulation has a U-value of 0.7-1.0 W/m²·K. Fitting 100 mm of
between the joists brings this to 0.2-0.25 W/m²·K. For a 20 m² living room, that is a reduction from roughly 550 W of floor heat loss (at design conditions) to under 140 W.

attrs.title: Safety before entry
attrs.body: Working in a subfloor void is confined-space work. Check for services (gas pipes, electrical cables) before entering. Wear a P2 dust mask, mineral wool fibres irritate the airways. Wear long sleeves and gloves; fibres irritate skin. Do not block or seal airbricks, the void needs cross-ventilation to prevent joist rot.

content.text: Remove an external airbrick and measure the void height. If less than 400 mm, access from below is not feasible and the batts must be fitted from above by lifting floorboards. At 400 mm or more, a slim adult can work from below.
Step 2, inspect for damp and rot.

Look for soft or discoloured timber on the joists before committing to insulation. Wet joists indicate either a drainage problem or blocked airbricks, insulating over an existing damp problem will make it worse. Resolve moisture first.

Measure the clear gap between joists at several points. Victorian floors are typically at 400 mm centres; Edwardian at 450 mm. Buy batts slightly wider than the gap (by 20-25 mm) so they friction-fit without sagging.
Step 4, push batts up between joists.

Cut batts slightly longer than the joist bay (by 10 mm). Push each batt up so it presses against the underside of the floorboard above. The batt must contact the floor deck, an air gap between batt and floor negates most of the benefit.

Three options: (a) nail timber laths across joist undersides every 500 mm; (b) staple polypropylene netting to joist undersides (fastest for large areas); (c) cut timber noggins to wedge between joists every 600 mm. Option (b) needs a staple gun and is the quickest when working from below.

items.cause: Joist gap wider than batt width, or batt not pressing tightly against floor deck
items.fix: Add timber noggins or stapled netting. Check batt thickness matches the available void height.

items.symptom: Mould appearing on floorboards after insulating
items.cause: Airbricks partially blocked, reducing subfloor ventilation
items.fix: Clear all airbricks. There should be at least two airbricks per 15 m² of floor area on opposing walls for cross-ventilation.

items.symptom: Floor cold in patches despite insulation
items.cause: Timber joists bridging the insulation, the joist has a higher U-value than the batt
items.fix: Joist bridging is unavoidable from below alone. If access from above is possible, an additional 25 mm PIR layer over the joists (under replacement floorboards) addresses bridging.
